On the eighth day, the old way of preparation ends and the new priesthood begins. Aaron stands before the altar not as a priest already assured of his role, but as a man who must first atone for himself. This detail cuts against our modern instinct to imagine priests as spiritually superior to those they serve. Aaron cannot approach God's presence without a sin offering. He cannot intercede for others without first being reconciled himself. He slaughters the calf, applies its blood to the horns of the altar, burns the prescribed portions—methodically, carefully, as Moses has commanded. Every step matters because God's presence is not casual. The people are told explicitly: "The LORD will appear to you today." This is not metaphorical. Something real is about to happen, and it will depend on whether the sacrifices are made correctly, whether Aaron obeys, whether the separation between the holy and the common is maintained.

What follows is a cascade of offerings—for Aaron, for the people, for atonement, for worship, for thanksgiving. The repetition in these verses is not tedious accident but the texture of serious worship. Each animal is brought, each piece is handled according to regulation, each portion goes where it belongs. The kidneys, the fat, the liver—these are not random details but evidence that the whole offering, inside and out, belongs to God. Aaron washes the entrails and legs himself; he washes what is hidden. Nothing is hidden from God, and nothing offered to Him is too mundane for reverence. Then Aaron approaches the people and blesses them, not from his own authority, but as a vessel of God's blessing. He has done the work. He has made atonement. Now he can pronounce words of peace over those whose sins have been covered.

But the real moment comes when Moses and Aaron emerge from the Tent of Meeting, bless the people, and fire descends from heaven and consumes the burnt offering and the fat portions. The people do not merely hear that God is pleased. They see it. The fire from the LORD's presence testifies that the sacrifices have been accepted, that the priesthood is established, that access to God through prescribed means is now real. The people shout for joy and fall facedown—trembling and celebrating at once, because they have witnessed what it means for a holy God to dwell among His people. This is not safe. This is not comfortable. This is the overwhelming reality that comes when the boundary between heaven and earth grows thin, when God's glory is not abstract doctrine but consuming fire.

The chapter calls us to recognize that approach to God has always been serious work. It has never been casual. The very precision that governed Aaron's movements—down to which parts of the animal belonged where—arose not from divine arbitrariness but from the weight of standing before a holy God. Christ fulfilled what these sacrifices pointed toward, absorbing in Himself the judgment and making the way open. But that does not make His cross less serious, less costly, less central to everything. If a single animal offering required such careful attention, such exact obedience, such fear and reverence, what should our response be to the One who offered Himself once for all? The fire of God's presence has not diminished. It has been satisfied. And that changes everything about how we approach Him.
